role_summary: Schoolteacher at Marshall High School in Belzoni, Mississippi and later in Tchula, Mississippi (Holmes County); mother of one of the children who attempted to integrate the Tchula public school in 1965 (the school was burned down twice in response); later elected to the Tchula school board.
ai_reading: Burns's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 78), with Robert G. Clark Jr. (entry 107, the first African American in the Mississippi House since Reconstruction and also a Holmes County native) as the top match and the broader cluster including Rosie Head (entry 111), Walter Bruce (entry 123, also Holmes County), and Euvester Simpson (entry 41). The Clark and Bruce pairings are the catalog's clearest Holmes County, Mississippi sustained-organizing-tradition cosines: Burns, Bruce, and Clark all worked the same county's voter-registration and school-integration arc, and Clark himself emerged from that organizing tradition into the Mississippi legislature. The novel observation her page surfaces is the teacher-as-organizer-under-job-threat signature: Burns describes how her teaching position at Marshall High School was threatened when she associated with SNCC members, the catalog's clearest documentation of the economic-retaliation pressure on Black professionals during Mississippi civil rights organizing, distinct from the front-line-organizer mode.
biographical_paragraph: While Burns's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 70 [src: 1]. Burns grew up in segregated schools in Humphreys County, Mississippi, and became a schoolteacher at Marshall High School in Belzoni, Mississippi (the Humphreys County seat). She describes how she took notice of the civil rights movement but limited her direct involvement because of the risk to her teaching job; her husband was active in civil rights work and her own job was threatened when she associated with SNCC members [src: 1]. In 1965 her son and other African American children attempted to integrate the white school in Tchula, Mississippi (Holmes County); the school was burned down twice by segregationists, and Burns documents both the protests by white residents against the integration and her own subsequent experience as a teacher in Tchula. She later won election to the Tchula school board and worked from that institutional position [src: 1].

sources.title: Julia Matilda Burns oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
